desc:
This hilltop is almost entirely flat, curving downward only slightly to the west
and east until it suddenly falls away in sharp cliffs. A deep layer of soil has
managed to accumulate here, and now supports a dense carpet of low, coarse
grass. A few pale-yellow oxlips and white orchids rise above the grass. A faint
path vanishes to the north, but becomes more distinct to the south.
name: Broken Hilltop

desc:
The floor and walls are made of polished stone in various shades of white and
grey. A frayed, red carpet covers the centre of the room from the entrance
towards a large, spiral staircase at the opposite end. On the eastern edge of
the room stands a large and sturdy wooden table. A black, dusty, iron-wrought
candelabra hangs high above, affixed firmly to the apex of the ceiling.
name: Main Hall of the Tower

desc:
Halfway up the wide staircase, a minstrel's gallery has been built overlooking
the main hall. If grand feasts were held below, the musicians might have
gathered here to play for the guests. The ominous creaking and groaning that now
comes from the wooden platform suggests that it hasn't aged all that well. Wide
stone steps lead up through a hatch to the bastion of the tower, or back down to
the main hall.
name: Wide Staircase

desc:
Once filled with the scents and sounds of a busy kitchen, this wide room now
stands cold and silent. All items of value that weren't too heavy to move have
been stripped out, leaving only the cold flagstones and an old, rusty stove.
Above the stove hangs a line of pegs that kept the tools of the trade within
easy reach of the cooks. A wide stone staircase leads upwards from the centre of
the room, next to which sits two massive iron cauldrons. Towards the back of the
kitchen, a long, dark corridor stretches out into the distance.
name: Kitchen below the Tower

desc:
This rather narrow passage is comprised of smooth, grey stone walls. A thick
layer of green, slimey lichen clings to the walls where moisture has seeped
through the cracks. Upon the floor lies broken pieces of pottery, stained
different colours from the various seasonings they once likely held. To the
east, the remnants of an old, disused kitchen can be seen, while to the west the
dank corridor continues until it is swallowed up in darkness.
name: Stone-walled Corridor

desc:
A narrow, stone corridor is lined with empty torch holders that once lit this
passage. Trickles of water run down the walls where cracks in the limestone have
not been repaired. A steady stream of cool air flows through cracks in the
western wall, the outline of which slightly resembles the frame of a door.
name: Stone-walled Corridor

desc:
Here the corridor turns and opens out from the narrow corridor to the north to a
large room to the east. Polished stones with deep grooves worn into the floor
underneath the dust show that, in the past, heavy items have been dragged
through here. The walls are composed of large square blocks of smooth grey
stone.
name: Stone-walled Corridor

desc:
This room was apparently an armoury at one time. Racks, hooks, and pegs that
line the wall seem to have been made to hold various types of arms and armour
but now stand empty, save for thick layers of dust collected during years of
neglect. In the southern corner, a pile of storage chests have collapsed, their
contents lost beneath a layer of rubble.
name: Plundered Armoury

desc:
This room appears to have been used as a smithy once, with a furnace located
deep within the basement to spread heat up through the tower. Blackened markings
on the walls and crumbled stone indicate the intense heat the furnace produced.
Amongst the recognisable remains in the room are an anvil and a selection of
hammers and tongs.
name: Abandoned Forge

desc:
High above the surrounding lands, this turret provides a bird's eye view of the
neighbouring countryside. A tall parapet made of hard, grey rock provides the
watchers with protection from any missiles that might be fired at them. To the
south, an old cobblestone path leads to and from the tower along a rocky
escarpment. To the east, a shimmering creek weaves its way through the
grasslands like a silvery thread upon a lush green blanket.
name: Top of a Tower

desc:
This room is plain, verging on the austere; the occupant obviously valued
function over comfort and had no time for ornamentation of any sort. Facing the
door is a battered but serviceable oak desk, the drawers stuffed with crumbling
parchment. The few remaining legible scraps indicate that the scrolls were
military maps of the area. A long rack set into the northern wall held pegs from
which various weapons were hung, ready to be issued should the tower come under
attack.
name: Guard Commander's Room

desc:
A large number of people seem to come and go frequently here, as the grass has
been heavily trampled. There appear to be three main paths. One leads north to a
large, crudely-crafted tent. A second path leads south to a wooden hut that has
certainly seen better days. The final path extends to the east, where a larger
grassy area opens up. A bare stone hillside drops away to the west.
name: Trampled Grass

desc:
This is the southern end of the hilltop, beyond which the hillside drops away in
an almost vertical cliff. The heavy grass present north of here nearly vanishes,
to be replaced by piles of boulders and smaller stones. The base rock of the
hill is visible, and is pierced in many places by narrow fissures. A very old
hut stands just to the west, though it looks like it may not stand for much
longer.
name: Jumbled Rocks

desc:
Short, thick-stemmed grass dominates this mostly flat area, with a sprinkling of
purple and lavender flowers. A single tall oak stands in the centre of the
grass, providing a large patch of shade on sunny days. There are a couple of
crude wooden benches set up at the base of the tree, and many tracks through the
grass lead both to the tree, to the west, and to a large tent that stands at the
north edge of the field.
name: Tall Oak

desc:
This one-room hut looks like it has stood here for decades. It may once have
been strongly constructed, but time has taken its toll. The log walls are
rotting and in many places coating with emerald moss, while the breeze flows
freely through a number of cracks. The roof appears to be intact, though the
support beams are sagging and could collapse at almost any time. Large holes
gape open in both the north and east walls, the doors that once filled them long
since vanished.
name: Deteriorating Hut

desc:
This tent is large but crudely fashioned, patched together from a number of
different pieces of fabric. The stitching is poorly done, and it seems almost
certain that the tent leaks in even the lightest rain. A haphazard collection of
wooden poles supports the ungainly structure. Several bedrolls, all of them
quite filthy, line the walls of the tent.
name: Crude Tent

desc:
This tent is quite extensive for a temporary shelter, and is clearly not meant
to be moved frequently. Oak poles and posts support a thick canvas roof and
sides, spreading the fabric out and leaving ample headroom for even the tallest
man. There is no floor to the tent, and the grass underfoot is long since dead
from lack of sunlight. Despite its ability to house many men, the tent contains
only a single cot, along with a chest, a small folding table, and a pair of low
stools.
name: Inside a Large Tent

desc:
No more than ten feet across at most points, this cave runs roughly east and
west. The eastern end is partially plugged with thick brambles, though it is
likely possible to push through them without too much trouble. The rock walls
are rough and the floor uneven, and although the ceiling is nearly flat it is
clearly that way naturally. The cave widens out suddenly to the west.
name: Narrow Cave

desc:
The cave is quite wide here and forms a T-shaped intersection with passages to
the north, south, and east. The eastern branch is the narrowest, while to the
south the cave appears to enter a large chamber. Layers of rock have fallen from
the ceiling, and the floor is littered with giant slabs intermixed with smaller
shards of stone.
name: Broad Cave

desc:
This cave is not very wide and snakes its way back and forth as it progresses.
It is straighter and wider to the south, while towards the north end it takes a
sudden turn eastward. Sharp projections abound on both walls, making it more
difficult to move without injury. The floor is roughly even, but it slopes a
little to the west, while the ceiling overhead is curved in a gentle arch.
name: Winding Cave

desc:
The cave comes to an abrupt end here, widening only slightly before doing so. It
appears that the tunnel once extended further east, but the way is now entirely
blocked by massive slabs and boulders. It would take a major mining effort to
clear the way. The walls are studded with countless flecks of mica and quartz
that twinkle when lit.
name: Dead End

desc:
The walls of this natural cavern are made of cold, hard soil. Tiny roots sprout
from the walls, covering the damp surface in a natural webbing. Drops of water
drip constantly from the ceiling, falling softly upon the floor to form small,
dark puddles. Towards the east, the dirt walls begin to bear the markings of
shovels and picks; long, deep swaths have been cut into the earth, slowly fading
into the darkening gloom as the tunnel advances. As the tunnel burrows deeper,
the earth and hard soil is gradually replaced by bedrock.
name: Damp Cavern

desc:
The ceiling of this damp tunnel slopes sharply towards the west. Grooves have
been scored at various depths into the walls and ceiling, evident that this
small underground cave was made by unnatural means. Upon the ground, broken
rocks and large clumps of hard soil lie strewn about, all bearing the marks left
by vigorous application of pick and shovel upon earth.
name: Dark, Rocky Tunnel

desc:
This portion of the hilltop is largely flat, with only a few rises and dips. The
area is covered with short but dense grass that has been heavily trampled. A few
tents and crude buildings stand here and there around the hilltop. There are a
dozen paths crossing one another seemingly at random, though most of them seem
to lead in the cardinal directions. The level area extends both east and west,
while becoming more rocky and broken to the north and south.
name: Bandit Camp

desc:
This looks like it is primarily a natural cave, though there are signs that it
has been expanded slightly and the floor has been leveled. A rickety ladder
rises against one wall, making it easy to return to the surface. Several old
crates line the walls; many are clearly empty, but a few may contain something
of value.
name: Hidden Storage Cavern

desc:
An underground stream pushes out from a crack on the dark rock wall. The water
splashes down to a small dark pool, then continues streaming southwards across a
twisting rocky cavern. Green algae covers the moist walls here. At one time the
current must have been stronger, for the cavern has been hollowed out enough to
allow for the passage of creatures.
name: An Underground Stream

desc:
A stream that flows from somewhere to the north of here spills into a large dark
lake, which stretches off both south and east. The surface of the lake is
disturbed by small ripples. A number of boulders have broken loose from the
ceiling and impede the path of the stream in places. There is a small path next
to the stream leading to the north.
name: By an Underground Stream

desc:
The dark water of the lake stretches off to the north, south and east, the
surface disturbed only by the occasional ripple which can be seen rebounding
from the rocky walls of the cavern. In the south-east a faint glimpse of land
can be seen.
name: Underground Lake

desc:
Water flows into this underground lake from a small stream to the west. The
surface of the lake is for the most part dark and still, but the occasional
ripple disturbs its surface, as though some underwater creature is moving about
stealthily. The lake stretches far to the south.
name: Underground Lake

desc:
The surface of the lake lies still and dark, disturbed only occasionally by
small ripples. To the east the lake laps up against a shallow rock shelf which
seems to be the beginning of a cavern. To the north the lake continues into
darkness.
name: Underground Lake

desc:
To the north and west a rocky shelf slopes gently into the dark waters of an
underground lake, while above and all around the rock walls form a natural
tunnel of stone, cut from the rock by the passage of water. Green slime and
algae grow on the walls in this damp atmosphere. The tunnel stretches out to the
east.
name: Robbers Haven

desc:
This room seems to be a naturally formed cavern, with the light from a torch
hanging in an ordinary iron bracket reflecting brightly from the wet, glistening
stonewalls. To the east the roof lowers, and there seems to be a smaller cavern
in that direction. To the east the room opens out.
name: Robbers Haven

desc:
This room looks like a small storage chamber. The walls drip with water, no
doubt due to the dampness of the air down here, and this seems to have affected
the food and other items stored here. Flour sacks and mouldy bread lie scattered
about the floor, making the rock underfoot slippery with a foetid slime.
name: A Small, Musty Chamber

desc:
The waters of the lake lap against a shallow horseshoe shaped rock shelf to the
south, from where the lake radiates out north and west. This shelf seems to form
a sort of beach, and looks like it is the entrance to a larger cavern behind. To
the north and west the lake continues into darkness.
name: Underground Lake

desc:
Hidden into the dark shadows of the cavern's upper parts, a small passageway
runs stealthily along the cavern walls. In many parts the pathway follows the
natural shapes of the soil, whereas in some places it looks that the way through
has been built on purpose. At the lower end, the passageway ends at a trapdoor.
At the upper end, the tunnel elevates onwards to the darkness. Along the way,
there is a skull lying in a niche in the passageway.
name: Small Passage

desc:
This cramped chamber has a low arched ceiling and a dirty appearance; the dingy
walls are covered with greenish mould. Rusted hoops and pieces of wooden staves
clutter a damp floor of large, roughly cut granite slabs. Some grisly looking
metal hooks hang from the ceiling.
name: Cramped Chamber

desc:
This farmhouse was ravaged by a fire in the past. Most of the roof is gone, and
what were once walls now lie in haphazard piles of blackened wood upon the
ground. Near the north-western corner, a gaping hole leads down into an old
cellar. The remains of an old hatch has long since rotted away and collapsed
into the darkness below. The only way to safely leave this ruin is through the
empty, skeletal door frame to the south.
name: Ruined Farmhouse

desc:
This cellar is small and cramped. It's square in shape and no more than ten feet
across. It's further restricted by rows of wooden shelves on the walls.
Everything here is coated in a thick layer of dust and soot. Broken glass lies
on the floor beneath a cracked, broken shelf.
name: Root Cellar

desc:
This narrow dead end is draped in old cobwebs. The air is dry and dusty and the
floor is covered in a thick layer of grime. The walls are close together and
there are no shelves to either side, except for an old, empty wooden rack that
stands against the back wall. To the east there is a slightly larger cellar with
a set of stairs that leads up through a hatch in the ceiling.
name: Narrow Niche

desc:
This small cellar room has a floor and walls of stone. The stones are fixed in
place with clay and great care was taken to make the floor even and the walls
smooth. Several simple chests and crates line the eastern wall. The only exit
from this room is a narrow opening in the northern wall.
name: Hidden Storage Room

desc:
The surrounding branches grow from the trunk of this large tree in unusual
fashion; where normally the branches are thin and dispersed numerous feet apart,
these grow thick and close together, forming a natural platform of sorts. The
thick branches below follow suit in a semi-circular pattern from just feet off
the ground, slowly circling upwards around the trunk of the tree. With the
exception of a brief, sparse tangle of intertwined branches just below, even the
most unskilled climber would have little trouble navigating the boughs.
name: Thick Branches

desc:
What was once a stately and dignified study for the tower's master now lies in a
state of disarray. Paint has begun to chip away from the fine wood panels, and
strips of ripped fabric are all that remains of the once-rich tapestries that
hung upon the walls. A tall window in the southern wall gives a broad view of
the path leading to and from the tower gate.
name: Wood-panelled Sitting Room

desc:
A narrow tunnel has been dug here, burrowing through the hill. It's dug at an
angle, creating a sloping passage that is difficult to navigate. At times the
tunnel makes a tight curve to bypass an obstacle. In a few places, small ladders
have been installed on the pathway to enable getting past vertical rock walls.
In some places the ground is smooth rock and in some places, rough gravel.
name: Escape Passage
